title: Access Level Sizing Guide
summary: Use this guide to understand how user roles, areas, schedules, door groups, and complexity settings combine into long-term access-control administration pressure.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: Access Level Sizing estimates whether an access-control permission structure will remain manageable as the system grows.
The tool models how user roles, areas, schedules, door groups, and complexity assumptions combine into access-level pressure, role-area combinations, scaling pressure, and administrative load.
This is the final step in the Access Control guided design flow. It does not size hardware directly; it checks whether the access structure itself is becoming too complex to support cleanly.

sections.title: When to use it
bullets: Before building access levels in the access-control platform.
Before a large expansion, migration, or site rollout.
When a system has too many one-off exceptions and is becoming hard to audit.
When roles, areas, schedules, and door groups are being designed by different stakeholders.
When validating whether the final access model is simple enough to support after installation.
When documenting why a design should be simplified before it turns into permission sprawl.

links.label: Credential Format Helper guide
links.note: Use this related guide when user population or credential lifecycle affects how large the access structure may become.

links.label: Panel Capacity guide
links.note: Use this related guide when the access model needs to be checked against controller and expansion planning assumptions.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These headings match the visible input labels on the calculator so the guide can be read directly against the form.

items.label: User Roles
items.description: The number of user groups with meaningfully different access needs. A role should represent an operational group, not every individual exception.
items.whyItMatters: Every additional role can multiply permission combinations across areas, schedules, and door groups. Role count is one of the strongest drivers of long-term administrative complexity.
items.commonMistake: Creating a new role for every small exception instead of grouping users by real operational need.

links.label: Credential Format Helper guide
links.note: Use this related guide when user population and credential lifecycle may affect role planning.

items.label: Areas
items.description: The number of logical zones, departments, or access regions that require separate permission treatment.
items.whyItMatters: Areas multiply against user roles. More areas may be necessary, but over-segmentation can create access levels that are difficult to explain, audit, and maintain.
items.commonMistake: Splitting the building into too many small zones when broader access areas would meet the actual operational need.

items.label: Schedules
items.description: The number of time windows or access schedules that control when access is allowed. Schedules add time-based logic on top of role and area permissions.
items.whyItMatters: Schedules make access behavior harder to predict and troubleshoot. Too many overlapping schedules can create confusion for users and administrators.
items.commonMistake: Creating many slightly different schedules instead of a small set of clear operating windows.

items.label: Door Groups
items.description: Sets of doors managed together because they share similar access behavior. Door groups simplify administration when used deliberately.
items.whyItMatters: Door groups can reduce repetitive programming, but too many groups can become another layer of complexity that must be named, documented, and audited.
items.commonMistake: Creating a separate group for every small exception instead of keeping a clean baseline with limited special cases.

links.label: Door Count Planner guide
links.note: Use this related guide when door quantity and segmentation affect the access structure.

items.label: Complexity
items.description: A planning adjustment for how complicated the access model is beyond the raw role, area, schedule, and door-group counts.
examples: Simple: clean structure, few exceptions, predictable schedules.
Normal: typical business access model with moderate grouping and schedules.
Complex: many exceptions, overlapping departments, mixed schedules, tenant separation, or special operating rules.
items.whyItMatters: Two systems with the same counts can be very different to support. Complexity accounts for the difference between clean intentional structure and messy exception-heavy structure.
items.commonMistake: Leaving the setting at Normal even when the actual system is full of exceptions, temporary users, after-hours rules, and overlapping responsibilities.

sections.title: How to interpret results
body: The result is a maintainability check. It estimates whether the access model is likely to remain understandable, auditable, and supportable.
A high access-level count does not automatically mean the design is impossible, but it does mean administration, naming, auditing, and troubleshooting will require more discipline.
bullets: Access Levels: the modeled permission structure size after schedule, group, and complexity pressure are applied.
Role-Area Combinations: the base relationship between user roles and controlled areas.
Scaling Pressure: how much complexity exists relative to the number of roles and areas.
Admin Load Index: a simplified indicator for day-to-day management pressure.
Recommended Limit: the planning threshold for the selected complexity setting.
Threshold Check: whether the modeled structure is under or over the recommended limit.
Overshoot: how far the design exceeds the recommended limit, if applicable.

sections.title: Healthy / Watch / Risk interpretation
body: The status describes administrative complexity pressure, not whether the access-control platform can technically support the configuration.
A platform may allow a large number of access levels, but that does not mean the design will be easy to support, audit, or explain.
bullets: HEALTHY: the structure appears likely to scale cleanly with manageable administrative overhead.
WATCH: the structure is workable, but future expansion could make administration fragile unless naming, grouping, and schedules are controlled.
RISK: the structure is likely to become administratively heavy, prone to assignment errors, and difficult to troubleshoot.

sections.title: Permission sprawl explained
body: Permission sprawl happens when access rules grow faster than the organization can understand or maintain them.
It usually starts with harmless exceptions: one special schedule, one extra role, one unique door group, or one temporary rule. Over time, those exceptions become the actual system.
This tool helps catch that pressure before the access-control platform becomes difficult to audit or support.
bullets: Too many roles create unclear responsibility boundaries.
Too many areas create excessive role-area combinations.
Too many schedules create unpredictable access behavior.
Too many door groups make permissions harder to explain.
Too many exceptions make audits slower and less reliable.

sections.title: Real-world planning workflow
bullets: Start with broad operational roles, not individual exceptions.
Define areas based on real access boundaries, not every room name.
Keep schedules limited and clearly named.
Use door groups to simplify repeated behavior, not to hide special cases.
Set Complexity honestly based on how many exceptions and overlapping rules the site will carry.
Run the analysis and simplify before building the access levels in the platform.
Document the final structure so administrators understand why the model was designed that way.

links.label: Door Count Planner guide
links.note: Use this related guide when physical door count and controlled-door scope influence access-level design.

links.label: Panel Capacity guide
links.note: Use this related guide when the access structure must align with controller and expansion planning.

sections.title: Simplification strategies
bullets: Combine roles that have nearly identical access needs.
Use fewer, clearer area definitions instead of many small zones.
Consolidate schedules into a limited set of standard operating windows.
Use door groups intentionally and name them clearly.
Separate temporary exceptions from permanent access rules.
Review whether high-complexity doors actually need unique treatment.
Build a naming convention before the system grows.

sections.title: Red flags
bullets: Every department wants a unique role.
Every room is treated as a separate area.
Many schedules differ by only a small time window.
Door groups are created to solve one-off exceptions.
No one can explain why a user has access to a specific area.
Access reviews depend on tribal knowledge instead of documented rules.
The platform technically supports the configuration, but administrators are already struggling to audit it.
Temporary contractors, visitors, or after-hours rules are handled as permanent access levels.

sections.title: Documentation checklist
bullets: User Roles and what each role represents.
Areas and the real-world boundaries they describe.
Schedules and when each one should be used.
Door Groups and which doors belong to each group.
Complexity setting and why it was selected.
Access Levels result and whether it exceeds the recommended limit.
Role-Area Combinations.
Admin Load Index and Scaling Pressure.
Simplification actions taken before deployment.
Known exceptions and who owns their review.

sections.title: Common mistakes
bullets: Creating roles for individuals instead of operational groups.
Treating every room or small space as a separate access area.
Using many overlapping schedules instead of a few clear windows.
Creating door groups without a naming standard.
Letting temporary exceptions become permanent rules.
Assuming platform maximums are the same as practical maintainability limits.
Skipping access-level documentation because the platform configuration seems self-explanatory.
Waiting until audit time to find out no one understands the permission model.

sections.title: Assumptions and limits
bullets: This tool models structural overhead and maintainability, not controller hardware limits.
Role and area counts are assumed to represent the active access model, not edge-case future scenarios only.
Schedule and door-group inputs are treated as meaningful contributors to administrative complexity.
The recommended limit is a planning threshold, not a manufacturer limit.
Outputs should be paired with site-specific operating policy, platform capabilities, administrative practice, and owner requirements.
Final access-control configuration should be validated against the actual platform, system administrator workflow, and audit requirements.
